summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on the death of his beloved Microsoft Natural Keyboard 4000, using it as a springboard to discuss what makes a good programming keyboard. He establishes three inviolable rules: never mangle the home key cluster, arrow keys, or function keys, as programmers depend heavily on these areas. Beyond these essentials, he acknowledges keyboard preferences are highly personal—ergonomic vs standard, clicky vs quiet, etc. He surveys popular options including the Microsoft Natural 4000, Das Keyboard, IBM Model M, and Apple's low-profile keyboard, ultimately arguing that a quality keyboard is one of the cheapest and best investments a programmer can make.
key_points: A quality keyboard is one of the best and cheapest career investments for programmers
Three keyboard areas are sacrosanct: the home key cluster, arrow key cluster, and function keys must never be non-standard
Programmers use function keys, home cluster, and arrow keys far more than average users, making standard layouts essential
Beyond the three rules, keyboard preference is highly personal (ergonomic, clicky, low-profile, etc.)
Keyboard switch technology was already mature in 1980—modern advances are mostly about wireless, aesthetics, and cost reduction
money_quotes: A quality keyboard is one of the best (and cheapest) investments you can make in your career.
These areas are sacrosanct for programmers.
Move those around and you might as well cut our fingers off while you're at it.
I don't know if this is God's way of punishing lapsed Visual Basic programmers, or what, but it's incredibly annoying.
Keyswitch technology was quite mature in 1980. Modern keyboard tech advances have mainly had to do with wireless interfaces, snazzy looks, and making cheap crud cheaper.
Give your keyboard the consideration it deserves; it is the one essential tool of our craft.
Pick whatever keyboard you like, as long as it's of reasonable quality, and you're comfortable typing on it for long periods.
key_insight: Programmers should invest in quality keyboards that respect standard layouts for function keys, home cluster, and arrow keys—the areas we use most intensively.
